<!-- prettier-ignore-start -->

# TS9004

**Category:** Typeclass Resolution · **Severity:** Error

## Message

```
@impl/@instance type must be a generic type reference (e.g., Show<Point>)
```

## Explanation

The @impl JSDoc tag or type annotation must reference a registered typeclass with a type argument.

Correct forms:
  /** @impl Show<Point> */   — typeclass Show, for type Point
  /** @impl Eq<User> */      — typeclass Eq, for type User
  /** @impl Ord<Date> */     — typeclass Ord, for type Date

Incorrect:
  /** @impl Point */         — not a typeclass reference
  /** @impl Show */          — missing the type argument

<!-- prettier-ignore-end -->
<!-- generated:end -->

## Example

```typescript
import type { Show } from "@typesugar/std";

interface Point {
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

/** @impl Point */
// error: TS9004 — @impl/@instance type must be a generic type reference (e.g., Show<Point>)
const showPoint: Show<Point> = {
  show: (p) => `(${p.x}, ${p.y})`,
};
```

## How to fix

The `@impl` tag must name the **typeclass applied to the target type** — both halves, as one generic type reference:

```typescript
import type { Show } from "@typesugar/std";

interface Point {
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

/** @impl Show<Point> */
const showPoint: Show<Point> = {
  show: (p) => `(${p.x}, ${p.y})`,
};
```

Writing only the type (`/** @impl Point */`) or only the typeclass (`/** @impl Show */`) fails the same way: the first has no typeclass to register under, the second has no type to register for. The legacy decorator form has the same requirement: `@impl("Show<Point>")`.
